MINI MINUTES FOR FEB-09-2024 09:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION 302, HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS COMERICA BANK, COMERICA INCORPORATED, AND COMERICA MANAGEMENT COMPANY, INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION IS HELD. MATTER ARGUED. AT THE CONCLUSION OF ARGUMENT, THE COURT TOOK THE MATTER UNDER SUBMISSION. JUDGE: ROCHELLE C. EAST. CLERK: JACQUELINE ALAMEDA. COURT REPORTER: RAQUEL SHARP, CSR #10619, TEL. 925.226.8222, EMAIL: RAQUEL@TRANSCRIPTLEGAL.COM. BAILIFF: DEPUTY JAIME. (301/RCE) *AFTER THE HEARING, THE COURT SIGNED AN ORDER GRANTING MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION (AND STAY ACTION). (SEE ORDER SIGNED THIS DATE FOR THE COURT'S COMPLETE RULING.)*

LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE APRIL-05-2024 HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF THE CALIFORNIA LABOR AND WORKFORCE DEVELOPMENT'S MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION OF THIS COURT'S ORDER OF FEBRUARY 9, 2024, TO COMPEL ARBITRATION OF PLAINTIFF'S INDIVIDUAL CLAIM AND STAY THE REPRESENTATIVE CLAIM IS DENIED. ORDER SIGNED (SEE SIGNED ORDER/MINI MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING). JUDGE: ROCHELLE C. EAST; CLERK: V. CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Nov-27-2024 Continued To Nov-12-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Motion For Reconsideration
Off Calendar Apr-05-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. MICHAEL CAMPBELL, Esq. of Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton LLP (tel: 310-228-3700) for Defendants.
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF THE CALIFORNIA LABOR AND WORKFORCE DEVELOPMENT's Motion For Reconsideration.

Matter not argued/contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for reconsideration of this court's order of February 9, 2024, to compel arbitration of plaintiff's individual claim and stay the representative claim is denied.

First, there is no basis for reconsideration because plaintiff does not cite "new law." Plaintiff cites the concurrence in Johnson v. Lowe's Home Centers, LLC, 93 F.4th 459 (9th Cir. Feb. 12, 2024) to argue that the court should revisit its decision to stay the representative action. The court has broad discretion to determine what constitutes "new law" for reconsideration. (Wilson v. La Jolla Group (2021) 61 Cal.App.5th 897, 921.) The court concludes that the musings of a concurring judge from a non-binding federal court opinion are not grounds for reconsideration. (People v. Superior Court (1976) 56 Cal.App.3d 191, 194 ["[a]concurring opinion does not constitute authority under the doctrine of stare decisis."].)

Second, even if the court were to reconsider its ruling, there is no basis to change it. The California Supreme Court has stated that a trial court "should" stay the representative action pending arbitration. (Adolph v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (2023) 14 Cal.5th 1104, 1124.) Even if the arbitration award has no preclusive effect, it may affect the outcome and settlement posture of the representative action, e.g., arbitrator determines that there was no Labor Code violation. The court also agrees that a stay conserves party and judicial resources and avoids conflicting rulings.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Court reporter: Jane Hong, CSR# 11975 (tel: 909-831-2644 / e-mail: csrjane@hotmail.com). (302/RCE)
